preamble: The transformation from a voiced retroflex stop /ɖ/ to a voiced alveolar flap /ɾ/ represents a common lenition process in phonology. This shift involves both a change in the manner of articulation, from a complete closure (stop) to a brief, ballistic contact (flap), and a change in the place of articulation, moving from the retroflex position (tongue tip curled back behind the alveolar ridge) to the alveolar ridge (deretroflexion). This makes the articulation less constricted and more anterior.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (stop to flap), Deretroflexion (retroflex to alveolar)

examples.note: The voiced retroflex stop /ɖ/ is realized as a voiced alveolar flap /ɾ/ intervocalically and in word-final position.

sources: De Jong, K. (2009). Explaining Coronal Reduction: Prosodic Structure and Articulatory Posture. Journal of Phonetics, 37(1), 1–18.

Kirchner, R. (1998). Lenition and the Perception of Prominence. In K. Shahin, S. Blake, & E. Kim (Eds.), Proceedings of the West Coast Conference on Formal Linguistics (WCCFL) 17 (pp. 333-347). Cascadilla Press.
